I treated the prior turns as external proposals. My read is that Turns 1â€“8 are genuinely refuted by the finite-tame tests recorded in the prior file, while the Turn 9â€“15 dynamic tame-inertia candidate remains the only serious survivor. The problem format is the four-generator tame format with distinguished sigma,tau, implicit tau^sigma=tau^2, and verifier exponents modulo M=85667662080.The prior fileâ€™s most important unresolved point is Turn 15â€™s warning: finite-tame DemuÅ¡kin data do not by themselves determine the 2-primary Frobenius action modulo U3.This is consistent with the general Jannsenâ€“Wingberg strategy, where one must control DemuÅ¡kin kernels over tame subextensions together with the tame action, not merely abstract kernel type.Numdam

The surviving candidate is still:variables sigma,tau,x,y definitions nx = (x*tau^50932630783)^40491355905 ny = (y*tau)^40491355905 relations x*sigma^45176306175*x*sigma^40491355905*x^-1*nx*ny^sigma*y^-1

Equivalently, with Ï€=Ï€2 and Î·=2Ï€ on tame inertia,r=xÏƒâˆ’Ï€xÏƒÏ€xâˆ’1(xÏ„âˆ’Î·)Ï€ ((yÏ„)Ï€)Ïƒyâˆ’1,with Ï€â‰¡40491355905,âˆ’Ï€â‰¡45176306175,âˆ’Î·=âˆ’2Ï€ â‰¡50932630783(modM).My new contribution is a more precise analysis of Turn 15â€™s Frobenius-action gap in the unramified quadratic case.Set Ï„=1, and pass to the unramified quadratic quotient, so Ïƒ maps to the nontrivial element of C2. Since Ï€â‰¡1(mod2), the relation specializes tor=xÏƒâˆ’1 xÏƒ[Ïƒ,y].

Let U=ker(Ïƒâ†¦C2), put S=Ïƒ2, and use Schreier generatorsx0=x,y0=y,x1=xÏƒ,y1=yÏƒ,S=Ïƒ2.

Reidemeisterâ€“Schreier rewriting gives the two relatorsr0=x0Sâˆ’1x1Sy1y0âˆ’1,r1=x1x0Sy0Sâˆ’1y1âˆ’1.

After abelianization over Z2, these givex0+x1+y1âˆ’y0=0,x0+x1+y0âˆ’y1=0.

Thus, witht=x0+x1,one obtains 2t=0,x1=âˆ’x0+t,y1=y0+t,and thereforeUabâ‰…Z2âŸ¨x0,y0,SâŸ©âŠ•C2âŸ¨tâŸ©.

Conjugation by Ïƒ, call it Î±, acts by Î±(S)=S,Î±(t)=t,Î±(x0)=âˆ’x0+t,Î±(y0)=y0+t.This is exactly the mod-4 Frobenius action one expects arithmetically. To see this concretely, let L/Q2 be the unramified quadratic extension, L=Q2 (Ï‰) withÏ‰2+Ï‰+1=0,F(Ï‰)=Ï‰2=âˆ’1âˆ’Ï‰.

In LÃ—/(LÃ—)4, take m=âˆ’1,u=1+2Ï‰,v=1+2(1+Ï‰),and the uniformizer 2. Then

F(u)=1+2Ï‰2=âˆ’1âˆ’2Ï‰=âˆ’u,while

F(v)=1+2(1+Ï‰2)=1âˆ’2Ï‰.

Also vF(v)=7, and in the stabilized quotient of OLÃ—modulo fourth powers one has 7â‰¡âˆ’1; for example, modulo 32,(3+6Ï‰)4 â‰¡âˆ’7.

Hence

F(u)â‰¡mu,F(v)â‰¡mvâˆ’1 (mod(LÃ—)4).So the candidateâ€™s abelianized action matches local class field theory under the identification

Sâ†”2,tâ†”âˆ’1,y0 â†”u,x0 â†”v.This directly addresses the concrete comparison proposed at the end of Turn 15.It also corrects the size of Turn 15â€™s ambiguity. Turn 15 computed a six-dimensional deformation spaceH1(C2,Hom(V,L2))â‰…F26 for the C2 -Frobenius action modulo U3. That is too large for the marked extension, because S=Ïƒ2 is not movable: any extension isomorphism fixing the distinguished tame generator Ïƒ must fix the marked element S. With

V=U/U2â‰…RâŠ•TbâŠ•TS,where R is the two-dimensional regular F2[C2]-module and Tb,TS are trivial, and withL2=U2/U3â‰…R3âŠ•T3,the marked condition is d(S)=0. Thus the relevant first-order ambiguity isH1(C2,Hom(RâŠ•Tb,L2))â‰…F23,not F26.Moreover, this three-dimensional ambiguity splits into a square-visible part and a purely commutator-valued part. The square-visible part is detected by the action on Uab/4, hence by the calculation above; it has dimension 2. After imposing the arithmetic mod-4 action, only one residual commutator-valued bit remains.

Concretely, choose degree-one basisa0=x0,a1=x1,b=y0+x1,c=S.

The quadratic DemuÅ¡kin initial relation isq=a0[2]+a1[2]+[b,c].

The remaining invisible bit is represented by the cocycle d(b)=[a0,a1],d(a0)=d(a1)=d(c)=0.

It is invisible in Uab/4, hence invisible to the local-class-field comparison just made. A Magnus rewrite using the group representative b=y0x1 shows that the candidate selects the nonzero value of this residual bit. I do not yet have an independent arithmetic verification of that last commutator bit.So the Turn 15 gap is real, but it is now much narrower: not a six-dimensional Frobenius-action ambiguity, but one commutator-only bit in the unramified quadratic U/U3 action after fixing S and matching LÃ—/(LÃ—)4.

ESTABLISHED: The Turn 9â€“15 candidate survives the unramified-quadratic mod-4 arithmetic check. Its action on Uab/4 matches the explicit Frobenius action on LÃ—/(LÃ—)4 for L/Q2 unramified quadratic. Turn 15â€™s six-dimensional deformation warning overcounts the marked problem; fixing S=Ïƒ2 reduces the ambiguity to F23, and the mod-4 arithmetic comparison fixes two of those three dimensions.

GAP: One commutator-valued Frobenius-action bit remains, represented by d(b)=[a0,a1]. The candidate appears to choose the nonzero value, but I have not yet matched that bit to an independent arithmetic invariant. The global dyadic formation proof is therefore still incomplete.

NEXT: Identify the remaining commutator bit arithmetically, likely via the C2 -equivariant structure of GL (2)/GL(2)3, or dually via a mod-2 triple/Massey-type invariant or an explicit descent calculation for small 2-extensions of the unramified quadratic field.[BREAKTHROUGH]
